content.text: Derived haemodynamic parameters convert raw pressures and cardiac output into clinically useful patterns. In CT-ICU they help identify cardiogenic shock, hypovolaemia, vasoplegia, RV failure, pulmonary hypertension and tamponade. They should be interpreted as patterns, not isolated numbers.
A normal MAP can coexist with dangerous low cardiac output if SVR is high. This is why cold, oliguric patients with a MAP of 70 still need escalation.

content.definition: Cardiac output adjusted for body surface area. It is the central flow number in many CT-ICU haemodynamic assessments.
The amount of blood ejected per beat. It falls with poor preload, poor contractility, excessive afterload, tamponade, severe valve dysfunction or RV failure.
The calculated resistance the LV/systemic circulation works against. It rises with vasoconstriction and falls with vasoplegia.
The calculated resistance the RV pumps against. It rises with hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis, pulmonary hypertension, PE, high airway pressure and pulmonary vasoconstriction.

content.text: SVR is calculated from pressure gradient divided by flow: (MAP - CVP) / CO x 80. PVR is calculated as (mean PA pressure - PAOP) / CO x 80. These formulae show why resistance can appear high when flow is very low, and why calculated values must be interpreted with the patient condition.

rows: PAOP often high if LV failure; CVP high if RV failure
LV failure after bypass, myocardial stunning, graft/ischaemia, valve dysfunction, arrhythmia.
Bleeding, vasodilation with inadequate volume, diuresis, third spacing.
Post-CPB inflammatory vasodilation, sepsis, ACE inhibitor effect.
Tamponade/clot, tension pneumothorax, massive PE, severe RV afterload.
CVP high, PA pressures/PVR high, PAOP normal/low unless LV disease
Pulmonary hypertension, hypoxia, acidosis, protamine reaction, RV infarct, high PEEP.

content.text: RVSWI and LVSWI estimate ventricular work per beat adjusted for body surface area. LVSWI falls with LV systolic failure or low filling. RVSWI falls in RV failure and can be misleadingly strained when PVR is high. These are advanced trend tools rather than single-decision values.

items: Start with flow: cardiac index and stroke volume.
Then assess pressure: MAP, CVP, PA pressures and wedge.
Then assess resistance: SVR and PVR.
Match the pattern to clinical markers: skin, mentation, urine output, lactate, SvO2, drains and echo.
Escalate if numbers suggest low flow even when MAP appears acceptable.

content.text: MAP is pressure, not flow. A clamped-down patient can have a reasonable MAP and terrible cardiac output.
Vasoplegia after bypass often has low diastolic pressure, warm peripheries and high vasopressor requirement. The treatment focus is vascular tone, not just fluid.
High CVP with low CI and high PVR is RV danger territory. Avoid hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis and excessive airway pressure.
Do not calculate SVR/PVR from bad inputs. A wrong wedge, wrong CVP level or inaccurate CO makes the derived value wrong with mathematical confidence.

Derived parameters are only as reliable as their inputs. The monitor may calculate CI, SVR and PVR automatically, but it requires correct height/weight or BSA, accurate CO, correct pressure transducer levelling, valid wedge and correct channel mapping.

items: Verify patient height/weight/BSA in the monitor if indexed values are used.
Confirm arterial, CVP and PA channels are labelled correctly and zeroed/levelled.
Check wedge validity before using PAOP in PVR or LV filling interpretation.
Check cardiac output method and quality before accepting CI/SVR/PVR.
If a derived number is surprising, audit the inputs before escalating therapy solely on the calculation.

rows: <2.2 with hypoperfusion = low cardiac output syndrome until proven otherwise.
Low SVI with high HR can hide poor beat-to-beat output.
<800 suggests vasoplegia; >1200 suggests vasoconstriction/compensation or excessive pressor.
High PVR strains RV; correct hypoxia, hypercarbia, acidosis and excessive airway pressure.
High suggests LV filling/pulmonary congestion if valid; low may support hypovolaemia.
High with low CI suggests RV failure/obstruction; low with low CI may be hypovolaemia.

quiz.question: A patient has MAP 75, CI 1.7, cool peripheries, lactate rising and SVR high. What is the main problem?
options: Adequate perfusion because MAP is normal
Low cardiac output with compensatory vasoconstriction
quiz.explanation: Pressure is preserved by high SVR, but flow is inadequate. CI and perfusion markers indicate low cardiac output.

quiz.question: What is the formula for SVR?
quiz.explanation: SVR is systemic pressure gradient divided by flow multiplied by 80.

quiz.question: Which pattern best fits vasoplegia after bypass?
options: Low SVR, low diastolic pressure, warm peripheries, vasopressor need
High SVR and cold peripheries always
High wedge and pulmonary oedema only
quiz.explanation: Vasoplegia is loss of vascular tone after CPB/inflammation, producing low SVR and often low diastolic pressure.
